reasoning: The St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index (STLFSI4) measures financial stress, with a historical mean of zero. The baseline value as of forecast time is extremely low (around -0.80 to -0.90), sitting in the bottom 16th percentile over the past year. Markets are highly calm, meaning the asymmetric risk of stress is sharply upward. Historically, in the past year, every time the index sank below -0.80, it increased in the immediately subsequent periods as markets exhibited slight mean reversion. Additionally, the FRED tool's linear trend model yields a 0.72 probability of increasing in the immediate next step. This strongly suggests the index will increase in the near term. As per the strict instruction that uncertainty must increase with horizon (pushing distant probabilities closer to 0.5), I step down the probabilities systematically for later resolution dates, even though mean reversion might normally imply a high sustained probability of being above an extreme low baseline.
